RESEARCH ARTICLE OPEN ACCESS 

# Glycoalkaloid-Free Starch Potatoes Generated by CRISPR/Cas9-Mediated Mutations of Genes in the Glycoalkaloid Biosynthesis Pathway Enable More Sustainable Uses of By-Products From Starch Production

Ying Liu1 | Irene Merino2 $\textcircled{1}$ | Mareike Gutensohn3 | Annika I. Johansson3 | Kalle Johansson4 Mariette Andersson1 | Per Hofvander1 $\textcircled { 1 0 }$ | Folke Sitbon2 

1Department of Plant Breeding, Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences, Lomma, Sweden | 2Department of Plant Biology, Uppsala BioCenter, Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences, Uppsala, Sweden | 3Swedish Metabolomics Centre, Department of Plant Physiology, Umeå University, Umeå, Sweden | 4Sveriges Stärkelseproducenter, Fjälkinge, Sweden 

Correspondence: Folke Sitbon (folke.sitbon@slu.se) 

Received: 27 June 2025 | Revised: 25 September 2025 | Accepted: 30 September 2025 

Funding: This work was funded by the SLU Grogrund project “Breeding of starch potatoes—improved quality for a more sustainable use of resources”. 

Keywords: CRISPR/Cas9 | potato (Solanum tuberosum) | potato protein | starch potatoes | steroidal glycoalkaloids | sustainable food production 

# ABSTRACT

Steroidal glycoalkaloids (SGAs) are toxic cholesterol-derived secondary metabolites present in several Solanaceae species. In potato, tuber SGA levels are for reasons of toxicity of concern in both table and starch cultivars. In the latter, SGAs bind to proteins and fibres in starch production side-streams and prevent their further uses as food and feed. To enable more sustainable uses of starch by-products, we have here reduced SGA biosynthesis in a starch potato cultivar using DNA-free CRISPR/Cas9. Six SGA genes were targeted, encoding enzymes acting either before cholesterol (SMO1-L, DWF1-L, DWF7-L), or after (16DOX, CYP88B1, TAMiso2). Editing efficiencies varied between $2 0 \%$ and $4 9 \%$ , and generated mutants were investigated under greenhouse and field conditions. Target mass-spectrometric analyses confirmed reduced SGA levels and alterations of sterol metabolism in mutated events. Plant height and tuber yield were reduced in several events, although this was not correlated to low SGA levels. Several knockout mutants had almost SGA-free leaves and tubers, the latter also under two SGA-inducing conditions. Similarly, both fibre and protein fractions isolated from side-streams in the starch production process from mutant tubers had very low SGA levels. By contrast, the corresponding wild-type SGA levels were almost 10-fold and, respectively, 40-fold higher than the recommended upper safe limit. The results demonstrate that glycoalkaloid-free mutants can be generated and grown with moderate yield reductions under both greenhouse and field conditions. This suggests a potential for sustainable production of high-value products, e.g., food-grade protein and fibre, from starch production side-streams of SGA knockout tubers. 

# 1 | Introduction

The potato (Solanum tuberosum L.) is today the fourth most important food crop species in the world. Potato tubers are mainly used as a staple food, but they also constitute a versatile raw 

material for several industrial applications, including starchrelated products, animal feed, alcohol and biofuel production. 

Steroidal alkaloids and their glycosylated forms; the steroidal glycoalkaloids (SGAs), are nitrogen-containing

$\circledcirc$ 2025 Sweden's Starch Producers Association and The Author(s). Plant Biotechnology Journal published by Society for Experimental Biology and The Association of Applied Biologists and John Wiley & Sons Ltd. 

secondary metabolites that are mainly present in plant species belonging to the Liliaceae and Solanaceae (Friedman et al. 1997; Ghisalberti 2006; Liu et al. 2024; Zhao et al. 2021). In cultivated potatoes, the predominant SGA forms are denoted $\propto$ -chaconine and $\propto$ -solanine, and together they constitute over $9 5 \%$ of the total glycoalkaloid (TGA) content in tubers. Wild potato species exhibit a broader SGA composition that includes a number of minor SGAs like demissine, leptine, solamargine, solamarine and solasonine (Friedman et al. 1997; Kozukue et al. 2008; Shakya and Navarre 2008). The TGA level in potato tubers is relatively low compared to that in other parts of the plant, but can increase significantly as a response to various conditions during growth and storage, such as wounding and light exposure (Friedman et al. 1997; Petersson, Arif, et al. 2013; Sinden et al. 1984). For food safety considerations, an upper level of $2 0 0 \mathrm { m g }$ TGA $\ k \mathrm { g ^ { - 1 } }$ fresh weight (f.w.) in raw, unpeeled, potato tubers is widely recommended (Schrenk et al. 2020). 

SGAs are composed of a hydrophobic $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 7 }$ skeleton and a sugar moiety attached to the C−3 position. The biosynthesis of SGA is initiated from acetyl-CoA in the mevalonate (MVA) pathway, leading to the formation of cholesterol, a direct precursor for SGAs (Ohyama et al. 2013; Petersson, Nahar, et al. 2013). The SGA pre-cholesterol pathway intersects with the biosynthesis of other sterols in the plant. A separation between sterol synthesis for housekeeping or SGA purposes is mediated through duplications of a small set of differentially regulated sterol biosynthetic genes, giving two parallel routes to cholesterol (Sonawane et al. 2016, 2020). The post-cholesterol part of SGA biosynthesis in potato involves several metabolic steps such as hydroxylation, oxidation and transamination to generate the aglycone solanidine. Subsequently, three glycosylations lead to structurally different SGAs (Cárdenas et al. 2015). A working model for the SGA biosynthesis via solanidine in potato plants is presented in Figure S1. 

While investigations of SGA levels in potato tubers mostly have been focussed on the safety of table potato cultivars, SGA levels are of interest also in starch potatoes. During potato starch production, substantial quantities of by-products are generated, including potato fruit juice (PFJ) and potato pulp. These by-products are typically discarded but can, as an alternative, be transformed into useful products. For instance, PFJ is rich in valuable compounds such as amino acids, minerals and proteins (Schoenbeck et  al.  2013). Potato tuber proteins stand out for their superior quality, marked by a high lysine content, compared to proteins from cereal sources (Waglay et al. 2014). Potato protein, with the properties of low allergenicity and high nutritional value, has the potential to substitute other plant- or animal-based proteins in food production (Hussain et al. 2021; Koppelman et al. 2002; McGill et al. 2013; Waglay et al. 2014). Potato pulp primarily comprises tuber cell wall materials and is abundant in pectin, presenting a potential source for the manufacture of high-value potato dietary fibre (Løkra and Strætkvern 2009; Meyer et al. 2009). However, high SGA levels in the residual by-products pose a significant challenge, making extraction of SGA-free proteins and fibres difficult without compromising their functional properties (Waglay et al. 2014). Various techniques for extraction, such as acidic precipitation and thermal coagulation, have been explored during the purification and functional recovery of potato proteins from 

PFJ (Cheng et  al.  2010; Miedzianka et  al.  2012; Schoenbeck et al. 2013; Waglay et al. 2014; Zwijnenberg et al. 2002). Yet, the functionality of proteins isolated via thermal treatment might be compromised, limiting their application to animal feed (Hussain et al. 2021; Schoenbeck et al. 2013). 

Studies in potato and tomato plants have demonstrated that down-regulation of various genes encoding enzymes acting in both the pre- and post-cholesterol pathways of SGA biosynthesis can lead to a significant reduction of SGA levels (Akiyama et al. 2023). Moreover, silencing of the AP2/ERF transcription factor GAME9/JRE4 has been shown to suppress SGA levels by lowering the expression of its target metabolic genes in the SGA biosynthesis pathway (Cárdenas et al. 2016; Nakayasu, Shioya, et al. 2018; Thagun et al. 2016). Traditional breeding approaches of potato face challenges due to the tetrasomic inheritance and high heterozygosity properties, resulting in a long and expensive process of breeding for achieving SGA reduction in tubers. However, advancements in modern breeding technologies, such as TALEN and CRISPR/Cas9 methods, facilitate the generation of low-SGA or even SGA-free potatoes by the possibility of targeting all alleles of key SGA metabolic genes. Pilot investigations of table potato cultivars using these techniques included induced mutations in the genes SSR2 (Sawai et al. 2014; Yasumoto et al. 2019, 2020; Zheng et al. 2021), 16DOX (Nakayasu, Akiyama, et al. 2018), and (Yasumoto et al. 2024). However, the efficiency of these approaches for significant reductions of SGA levels in tubers or tuber fractions has insofar been investigated only to a minor extent. 

While the above-mentioned studies have focused on the role of single genes for lowering SGA levels in table potatoes, there have been no corresponding investigations concerning the SGA reduction in starch potato cultivars. The SGA metabolism in starch potatoes has been little investigated, and there have been no investigations using targeted silencing of SGA genes in such cultivars. Nonetheless, the generation of low-SGA starch potatoes would present a valuable opportunity for industrial application and offer a novel and more sustainable approach to produce food-grade potato protein or fibre products from industrial by-products. 

With the long-term goal to create a more sustainable use of starch production by-products, we have here employed DNAfree CRISPR/Cas9 to target different genes acting in both the pre- and post-cholesterol parts of SGA biosynthesis in the starch potato cultivar ‘Kuras’. Generated mutants were analysed under both greenhouse and field conditions, and field-grown mutants were used for analyses of SGA levels in starch production side-streams. 

# 2 | Results

# 2.1 | Generation of Potato Events With Mutations in Key SGA Biosynthesis Genes Using DNA-Free CRISPR/Cas9

Aiming at the generation of SGA-free starch potato plants, six genes encoding enzymes with a role in the pre- or postcholesterol parts of the SGA biosynthesis pathway were targeted 

by CRISPR/Cas9-mediated mutagenesis. The genes targeted for induced mutations in the pre-cholesterol SGA pathway were DWF1-L (SSR2), SMO1-L (SMO3) and DWF7-L (C5-SD2); while the genes targeted in the post-cholesterol pathway were 16DOX (GAME11), CYP88B1 (GAME4, PGA3) and TAM isoform 2 (GAME12, PGA4) (Figure S1). The expression of these genes has been shown to be associated with between-cultivar differences in tuber SGA levels, and is upregulated in tubers before SGA levels increase as a response to wounding or light exposure (Nahar et al. 2017; Merino et al. 2023). Together this indicates a limiting role for the respective gene in SGA biosynthesis. 

For each targeted SGA gene, two sgRNAs targeting different exons were used in individual single guide experiments to generate mutants (Figure  S2, Table  S1). In addition, a doublegene mutagenesis strategy was undertaken for the early sterol biosynthesis genes SMO1-L and DWF1-L, in this case by multiplexing two sgRNAs in different target combinations. Mutations induced in the regenerated shoots were identified by highresolution fragment analysis (HRFA). Based on the results, the different mutation events were classed into three groups to describe the allelic dosage of mutations. Events were denoted as group 1 if indels were out of reading frame in all four alleles (i.e., full knockouts), or as group 2 if all alleles were mutated, but at least one allele had indels that potentially were in-frame. The group 3 events were partially mutated, having at least one unedited wild-type allele present. 

Including all genes targeted, 497 edited events were identified by HRFA from a total number of 1435 regenerated shoots (Table S2). The editing efficiency differed between the mutated genes and sgRNAs used, and ranged from $2 0 \%$ for SMO1-L and CYP88B1 to $4 9 \%$ for DWF7-L and TAMiso2 mutations. Out of the 497 identified edited events, 96 events were further analysed by Sanger DNA sequencing to confirm the induced mutations. HRFA chromatograms and corresponding confirmation of mutations by Sanger sequencing for representative pre-cholesterol and post-cholesterol mutant events are presented in Figures S3 and S4, respectively. 

# 2.2 | Initial Screening of Mutated Potato Events for Lowered SGA Levels

The 96 events were subjected to an initial screening of SGA levels. Care was taken to include at least 2–3 events for each mutation group. Exceptions to this were for CYP881B, where only a single full knockout event (group 1) was generated, SMO1-L where no full knockout and only one in-frame mutant (group 2) was obtained, and for the double-gene SMO1-L + DWF1-L mutants where no events with group 1 edits in both genes were identified (Table  S3). Plants grown in  vitro were analysed for their levels of $\propto$ -solanine and $\propto$ -chaconine levels using a semiquantitative thin-layer chromatographic (TLC) analysis. The results indicated a decreased SGA level for 30 out of 50 events $( 6 0 \% )$ for the pre-cholesterol mutants, and 32 out of 46 $( 7 0 \% )$ events in the post-cholesterol mutants (Table  S3). A low-SGA level was significantly more frequent among events from the mutation groups 1 and 2 than from group 3 ${ p < 0 . 0 0 0 1 }$ ; chisquare test), demonstrating the importance of four-allele edits to obtain a lowered SGA level. 

# 2.3 | General Appearance and Growth of Mutated Potato Events

From the TLC results, 39 events with low SGA levels and predominantly belonging to the mutation groups 1 or 2 were selected for analyses of growth and tuber yield under greenhouse conditions (Table  1). After 4 months, the height of 20 events $( 5 1 \% )$ was significantly shorter than that of the wild type, and an additional 3 events $( 8 \% )$ were unviable under the greenhouse conditions (7d032, 9d054 and 9c068) (Figure 1a, Figure S5). The tuber yield was also impacted, with reductions ranging from $2 7 \%$ to $8 7 \%$ compared to the wild type (Figure 1b, Figure S5). There was a strong correlation between reduced plant growth and a low tuber yield $\scriptstyle { p < 0 . 0 0 1 }$ ; ANOVA). The general appearance and growth of the remaining 16 events were normal (Figure 1c). This set of plants included 13 events that displayed very low or undetectable SGA levels. Further comparisons showed that neither the reduced plant size nor the lowered tuber yield were significantly correlated to the SGA level in leaves or in tubers from the chosen set of 36 events. Together this suggests that factors other than a lowered SGA level, such as somaclonal variation, contributed to the reduced growth of some events. 

# 2.4 | Development of a UHPLC–MS/MS SGA Analysis Method

The TLC screening indicated very low SGA levels in several events. To determine the low SGA levels more accurately, we developed a UHPLC–MS/MS analysis method using added $\mathrm { U } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } \cdot$ - labelled standards for both $\propto$ -chaconine and $\propto$ -solanine. 

Depending on the SGA species, the analytical precision ranged between $2 \%$ and $7 \%$ relative standard deviation (RSD) (Table S4). Repeated analyses of TGA levels (i.e., $\propto$ -solanine $+ \alpha$ -chaconine) in single wild-type tuber samples ranged within $\pm 1 0 \%$ RSD, depending on the sample SGA level (Table S5). For both $\propto$ -solanine and $\propto$ -chaconine, the limit of quantification (LOQ) was $0 . 5 \mathrm { p g } ,$ and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.05pg. This can be seen as being equivalent to quantification of ca. $0 . 5 \mathrm { m g }$ TGA $\ k \mathrm { g ^ { - 1 } }$ (f.w.), or detection of ca. $0 . 0 5 \mathrm { m g \ T G A k g ^ { - 1 } }$ $\mathrm { k g ^ { - 1 } }$ , in a standard extraction of a $2 5 \mathrm { m g }$ tuber sample. Considering that a normal TGA level in wild-type plants is about $1 5 0 0 \mathrm { m g }$ TGA $\ k \mathrm { g ^ { - 1 } }$ (f.w.) in leaves and $1 0 0 \mathrm { m g T G A k g ^ { - 1 } }$ (f.w.) in tubers, the method was thus well suited to quantify the lowered TGA levels present in mutated events. 

# 2.5 | Decreased SGA Levels in Pre- and Post-Cholesterol Mutated Events

To further analyse the effects of induced mutations on SGA metabolism, the TGA levels were quantified using UHPLC–MS/ MS in both leaves and tubers of the 36 selected events cultivated in the greenhouse. 

Analysis of leaves showed that the majority of events (29 out of 36; $8 0 \%$ ) had TGA levels that were below $1 0 \%$ of the wild-type level (Figure 2a, Figure S6a,b). The lowest leaf TGA levels were detected in the 16DOX mutants, which displayed an almost complete absence of both $\propto$ -chaconine and $\propto$ -solanine. Likewise, TAMiso2 mutants, the double mutants of SMO1 ${ \cdot } L + D W F I { - } L$ , and

the single full knockout of CYP88B1 had very low TGA levels. In the corresponding tuber samples, TGA levels were extensively reduced in all group 1 and group 2 events (Figure 2b, Figure S6c,d). A total of 16 events were identified that had tuber TGA levels less than $5 \%$ of the wild-type level. All five 16DOX events (group 1) displayed a near absence of TGA in both leaves and tubers. 

To investigate the mutant properties under SGA-inducing conditions, tubers from selected mutated events were subjected to wounding or light exposure treatments (Figure  2b, Figure S6c,d). The wild-type tubers displayed a statistically significant ca. 3-fold TGA increase after both wounding and light exposure treatments. By contrast, mutated events displayed no, or only a slight increase in the TGA level in the treated tubers, and the final TGA level was in almost all cases not statistically different from that of their corresponding untreated control, except for two CYP88B1 group 2 or 3 events. 

# 2.6 | Sterol Profiles in Pre- and Post-Cholesterol Mutated Events

To investigate the effect of SGA key gene mutation on sterol biosynthesis, a sterol profiling using GC–TOF–MS was conducted on leaf extracts from the mutated potato events. In total, 11 sterol species were identified by the analysis (Figure S7). 

Compared to the wild type, sterol levels were altered to some extent in most events. An altered profile was particularly evident in the pre-cholesterol mutants (Figure 3). Both cycloartenol and cycloartanol, two early precursors of cholesterol (a $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 7 }$ sterol and direct SGA precursor), displayed a strongly significant accumulation in the analysed SMO1-L event, together with an increased level of lanosterol. By contrast, the level of cholesterol was decreased, indicative of a block in the $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 7 }$ sterol pathway. Corroborating the results, a similar sterol profile was observed in the double mutants of SMO1-L+DWF1-L. Significant increases of the more downstream cholesterol precursors lophenol and lathosterol were detected in the DWF7-L events, and also this was associated with a corresponding cholesterol reduction. This sterol profile is well in line with lathosterol as a DWF7-L substrate in the cholesterol pathway (Figure S1). Cholesterol levels were significantly decreased also in DWF1-L events, although this was associated with increases in the $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 8 }$ and $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 9 }$ sterol pathways, including sterols such as cycloeucale-28 29nol, campesterol and isofucosterol, as well as one unidentified $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 8 }$ - like sterol with a tentative $\mathrm { m } / \mathrm { z }$ of 398. 

Thus, a reduction of cholesterol levels was significant in all pre-cholesterol mutants. Sterol changes in the post-cholesterol mutants were smaller and less significant but included lower cholesterol and stigmasterol levels in TAMiso2 and reduced levels of stigmasterol in CYP88B1. The 16DOX mutants were not significantly different from the wild type for any of the 12 sterols analysed. 

# 2.7 | Mutant Growth Under Field Conditions

To investigate the mutant performance also under field conditions, we performed a field experiment with one event from each 

of seven mutations. Four of the seven events were full knockouts (KOs), and one was a double SMO1- $. L + D W F { - } L$ mutant. All events were grown in parallel and considered common cultivation practice for the location. 

Compared to the wild type, all events showed a reduction in tuber yield (Table 2). The tuber yield per plant ranged from $3 \%$ (DWF7-L) to $8 1 \%$ (SMO1-L). The reduced yield was due both to fewer and smaller tubers (Table 2). Analyses of tuber TGA levels showed levels below $3 \%$ of the wild-type level for all events, except for the intermediate CYP88B1 group 2 mutant. The effect of wounding and light exposure on SGA accumulation in tubers from field conditions was similar to that in tubers grown under greenhouse conditions (Table 2). 

Similar to the tuber yield of greenhouse-grown plants, the lower tuber yield under field conditions was not significantly correlated to a low TGA level (ANOVA). For instance, the tuber yield per plant was $1 . 1 \mathrm { k g }$ for the DWF1-L event but only $0 . 2 3 \mathrm { k g }$ for TAMiso2, although the TGA levels were clearly higher in TAMiso2 $\mathrm { \Delta } [ 6 . 3 \mathrm { m g }$ TGA $\mathrm { k g ^ { - 1 } }$ vs. $3 . 1 \mathrm { m g }$ TGA $\mathrm { k g ^ { - 1 } } ,$ ). Moreover, the tuber yield under field conditions was not significantly correlated to the yield in the greenhouse, but there was a strong correlation between the corresponding TGA levels $\cdot p { < } 0 . 0 0 1$ ; ANOVA). These results suggest that tuber yield under field conditions may be affected by gene-specific effects that are unrelated to the TGA level as such. 

# 2.8 | Lowered SGA Levels in Starch Production Side-Streams

To investigate if tubers from the low-SGA mutated events could be used for the production of food-grade compounds from byproducts in a starch extraction process, the field-grown tubers were used as a starting material to isolate purified fractions of starch, fibre and protein. 

The starch content in mutants was similar to that in the wild type, or slightly reduced in 16DOX and TAMiso2; the events that also displayed the lowest tuber yield (Table  2). For the wild type, as well as for all mutant genotypes, TGA analyses of the pure starch fraction showed very low levels; below 3 mg TGA $\ k \mathrm { g ^ { - 1 } }$ d.w. (Figure 4). The wild-type fibre and protein fractions contained much higher levels, 1132 and $5 7 9 1 \mathrm { m g }$ TGA $\ k \mathsf { g } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively. Considering the recommended upper safe limit for potato proteins of 150 mg TGA $\ k \mathrm { g ^ { - 1 } }$ (Byrne 2002), this corresponds to levels 8-fold and 40-fold above the safe limit. By contrast, TGA levels in both the fibre and protein fractions were well below the safe limit in four or five of the six chosen lines for protein and fibre, respectively. The four mutants with a low TGA content in the protein fraction (7c028, 7f037, 9b055, 6b015) all contained group 1 events, again demonstrating that full KOs are more efficient for TGA reduction effects. The lowest TGA levels in fibre and protein fractions were detected in the 16DOX-mutated event, which contained a TGA level of $3 . 1 \mathrm { m g } \mathrm { k g } ^ { - 1 }$ d.w. in both fractions. This level corresponds to about $2 \%$ of the recommended safe limit, and is very similar to the low TGA level in the edible starch fractions, and more than 1600-fold lower than the TGA level in the wild-type protein fraction.

# 3 | Discussion

Here, we present results from a targeted mutagenesis of six key genes in SGA biosynthesis, using CRISPR/Cas9. The CRISPR method used results in transgene-free mutants (Andersson et al. 2018), a desirable feature considering present legislation in many countries. The SGA genes were selected based on a role in either the pre- or post-cholesterol parts of the SGA biosynthesis pathway. TLC analyses of 96 mutated events revealed a considerable TGA variation between events (Table S3). Further, TGA quantifications of 36 events using UHPLC–MS/MS showed that mutants had reduced TGA levels in both leaves and tubers, and that a number of events displayed close to glycoalkaloid-free tubers (Figure 2, Figure S6). Generally, mutation of all four alleles was necessary to achieve a strong TGA reduction. Mutations in three alleles were also efficient in some cases (e.g., 16DOX and TAMiso2), whereas only the full out-of-frame (group 1) mutant among CYP88B1 events displayed a significant TGA reduction (Figure  S6). Together, this variability suggests that the chosen key genes encode proteins that limit the SGA biosynthesis to different degrees. 

The general appearance and morphology of most mutated plants was similar to that of the wild type, although about $5 0 \%$ of the events displayed a reduced height and/or tuber yield (Figure 1, Table 2). The tuber yield reduction was seen during both greenhouse and field conditions and was somewhat more pronounced in the latter. However, there was no correlation between a reduced plant height or tuber yield and a reduced SGA content. Full knockout mutants with very low SGA levels together with a fully normal growth pattern were identified both for precholesterol events (DWF1-L; SMO1-L; DWF1-L + SMO1-L) and post-cholesterol (TAMiso2) events (Figures  S5 and S6). This aligns well with the description of normal phenotypes for low-SGA plants in previous studies (Umemoto et al. 2016; Nakayasu et  al.  2017, 2021). The fact that similar growth or yield effects were observed among events from mutation of all six key genes suggests that the observed phenotypic effects at least in part are due to random secondary effects, e.g., somaclonal variation, that are known to originate from plant micropropagation processes (Bairu et al. 2011; Ferreira et al. 2023). However, other explanations are also plausible, such as accumulation of harmful levels of substances as a result of the inhibited step. Reduced growth of

The tuber SGA level is known to increase under various environmental conditions (Friedman et al. 1997). Previous studies have shown that the SGA-inducing response to a wounding treatment or a light exposure varies significantly between table

potato cultivars (Petersson, Arif, et al. 2013; Nahar et al. 2017; Merino et al. 2023). The results obtained from a starch potato cultivar, ‘Kuras’, align well with these studies and indicate that starch cultivars have similar responses to SGA-inducing conditions as the table cultivars (Figure 2). Thus, post-harvest treatment and maintenance of starch potato tubers in the same way as is routinely done for table potatoes may serve as a simple first step of keeping SGA levels low in the starch potato cultivars. By contrast, the SGA-inducing effects of wounding or a constant light exposure in the group 1- or group 2-mutated events were insignificant. This was true both in tubers obtained from greenhouse as well as field growth conditions (Table  2, Figure  S6). This suggests that the different key SGA gene mutations interfere with both the basal and the induced SGA synthesis. 

Among the 36 mutated events that were selected for more indepth studies, a reduction of TGA levels was somewhat less efficient for group 1 mutants in the pre-cholesterol SGA pathway than in the post-cholesterol one (Figure S6). This result might be due to a less unique position for pre-cholesterol proteins in the SGA biosynthesis pathway. The chosen pre-cholesterol genes are known to exist in pairs, e.g., DWF1-L/DWF1, SMO1-L/SMO1 and DWF7-L/DWF7, with each copy predominantly participating in SGA (cholesterol) or housekeeping sterol biosynthesis (Sawai et al. 2014; Cárdenas et al. 2016; Sonawane et al. 2016). It can be hypothesised that when the chosen pre-cholesterol genes are knocked out, the duplicated counterparts are to some extent able to compensate for the knocked-out genes, thus maintaining a certain level of both sterols and SGAs in the mutants. In line with this are the more efficient TGA reductions observed in the double SMO1-L + DWF1-L mutants, compared to those in the single mutant counterparts. 

To our knowledge, a key role of SMO1-L and DWF7-L in the potato SGA biosynthesis is shown here for the first time. SMO1-L and DWF7-L were previously suggested as being associated with cholesterol biosynthesis based on co-expression with the SGA regulatory transcription factor GAME9 (Cárdenas et al. 2016). Here, both SMO1-L- and DWF7-L-mutated events displayed a significant decrease in SGA levels, confirming their involvement in the SGA biosynthesis pathway. The accumulation of lathosterol and reduction of cholesterol in the DWF7-L-mutated events (Figure 3) supports the position of DWF7-L in the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway (Figure  S1). However, the DWF7-L events were also associated with a decrease in the levels of sitosterol and stigmasterol, indicating that DWF7-L may to some extent contribute also to the $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 9 }$ sterol pathway. 

Likewise, the reduced levels of cholesterol and TGA in SMO1-L and DWF1-L events, as well as in the double SMO1-L + DWF1-L ones, demonstrate a role for these genes in the potato cholesterol and SGA biosynthesis. In addition to reduced levels of cholesterol, the sterol profiles of DWF1-L-mutated events (i.e., DWF1-L and SMO1- $. L + D W F I - L )$ ) were characterised by increased levels of $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 8 }$ and $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 9 }$ sterols, e.g., campesterol and isofucosterol. This parallels the sterol profiles of low-cholesterol potato plants where the SSR2 gene (i.e., DWF1- L) had been inactivated by TALEN technique (Sawai et al. 2014), as well as of transgenic potato plants overexpressing a heterologous sterol methyl transferase type 1 gene (SMT1) that directly increased the $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 8 }$ and $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 9 }$ sterol synthesis from cycloartenol (Arnqvist et al. 2003). Tubers were harvested after 5 months, and the sum of predominant SGA forms $\propto$ -solanine and $\propto$ -chaconine (TGA) was quantified using UHPLC–MS/MS in purified starch (a), and in the side-stream fractions fibre (b), and protein (c). n.d.; not done due to poor tuber yield. results are thus consistent with a re-direction of sterol metabolism in DWF1-L mutants from $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 7 }$ cholesterol biosynthesis to an increased synthesis of $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 8 }$ and $\mathrm { C } _ { 2 9 }$ sterols. By contrast, the events where the SMO1-L gene had been mutated (i.e., SMO1-L and SMO1-L + DWF1-L) displayed a different sterol profile characterised by an accumulation of lanosterol, cycloartenol, as well as cycloartanol. Taken together, the results show that mutations of SMO1- $L$ or DWF1-L affect sterol metabolism differently, and suggest that DWF1-L acts before SMO1-L in our SGA pathway model (Figure S1). 

The 16DOX-mutated events generated in our study displayed the lowest TGA levels among all mutants, and the tuber TGA level was close to the level of detection for our UHPLC–MS/MS method $( 0 . 1 \mathrm { m g } \mathrm { k g } ^ { - 1 }$ f.w.). 16DOX was the first 2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase (2OGD)-encoding gene identified with a role in the SGA biosynthesis pathway, in this case a steroid 16α hydroxylation (Nakayasu et  al.  2017). Our results are consistent with results from 16DOX-silenced potato plants and 16DOX-mutated potato hairy roots (Nakayasu et al. 2017; Nakayasu, Akiyama, et al. 2018). The efficient reduction of TGA levels in 16DOX mutants might be expected from the apparent single-gene status of 16DOX. Moreover, there were no significant effects of 16DOX mutations on sterol metabolism, indicating that the action of the 16DOX enzyme is specific for the SGA metabolic processes occurring after cholesterol. 

The TGA level in the single CYP88B1 (GAME4) out-of-frame knockout (event 9d050) was also close to zero, both in leaves and tubers, aligning with observations in iGAME4-silenced or CRISPR/Cas9 knockout potato plants (Akiyama et  al.  2017; Itkin et  al.  2013; Paudel et  al.  2017). However, 9d050 exhibited a dwarf phenotype and significantly lower tuber yield (Figure 1). This contrasted with the GAME4-silenced potato 

plants, which phenotypically resembled the wild-type plants (Itkin et  al.  2013). The observed phenotypic differences between these studies might be attributed to somaclonal variation and/or harmful accumulation of unidentified compounds. Nonetheless, our findings corroborate CYP88B1 as a key enzyme in SGA biosynthesis. Metabolic un-target analyses of RNAi-inhibited iGAME4 (CYP88B1) transformants indicated effects on the metabolism of several compounds, e.g., alkaloids, saponins and terpenoids (Paudel et  al.  2017). An extended analytical work is thus needed to show if this is the case also in our CYP88B1 mutants, as well as in the mutants of other SGA genes. 

In all nine TAMiso2 knockout events (mutation groups 1 and 2) analysed, the SGA levels were significantly reduced in both leaves and tubers, with a normal morphology of mutated plants and tubers when grown in the greenhouse. This was irrespective of whether the mutation position was in the early (exon 2) or later exon (exon 16) in the events. This indicates that TAMiso2 plays an important role in the SGA biosynthesis, aligning with the conclusions from PGA4- silenced potato lines reported by Nakayasu et  al.  (2021), TAMiso2-overexpressing transformants (Merino et  al.  2023) and GAME12 in vitro assays (Grzech et al.2024). TAMiso2 full knockout tubers had significantly lower TGA levels than the wild type, but the levels were ca. 100-fold higher than those in 16DOX mutants. TAM proteins belong to the family of gamma-aminobutyric acid transaminases (GABA-T) and are known to exist in different isoforms (Clark et  al.  2009). Recently, the cytosolic forms GABA-T2 (GAME12, PGA4) and GABA-T4 were shown to have SGA-forming activities in vitro, whereas the mitochondrial form GABA-T1 and the plastidic form GABA-T3 had not (Grzech et al. 2024). We speculate that a certain complementation of TAMiso2 events might occur 

from these isoforms, presumably mainly GABA-T4, in a way similar to the apparent complementation of pre-cholesterol gene mutants. Further, the TAMiso2 mutants were the only ones for which the TGA reduction was more efficient in leaves than in tubers, indicating that the expression or activity of complementing isoforms may differ between organs. Possibly, this type of difference among genes in their organ-dependent SGA reduction efficiency could be used to isolate mutants with a more desirable distribution of SGA levels in the plant, e.g., normal levels in leaves but low levels in tubers. More detailed analyses of the RNA expression or enzymatic activity of both target genes/enzymes and their related counterparts will now be needed to investigate this possibility, both regarding different TAM genes and other key SGA genes. 

There is today considerable evidence for a role of SGA in the interaction between potato plants and pathogens or pests (e.g., Chowanski et  al.  2016; Friedman et  al.  1997; Liu et  al.  2024). This research has more recently been extended to include also transgenic or mutant potato plants with altered SGA levels. For instance, RNAi-inhibited iGAME4 (CYP88B1) transformants were in comparison to wild-type leaves demonstrated to give a more rapid development of larvae from the Colorado potato beetle (Paudel et al. 2017). Moreover, leaves from knockout Solanum nigrum mutants of the GAME15 gene, encoding a cholesterol-metabolic and SGA scaffold protein, displayed strongly reduced SGA levels and an increased susceptibility to larvae from the generalist insect Spodoptera littoralis (Jozwiak et  al.  2024). In parallel research, Boccia et  al.  (2024) demonstrated that GAME15 knockout plants of S. nigrum lacked both SGA and steroidal saponins, and that the lack of saponins rather than SGA was related to an increased insect feeding. In our field experiment, however, we did not observe any signs of altered damage from pathogens or pests, although it should be remembered that the experiment was designed for analyses of plant growth rather than insect interaction. Nonetheless, the different pre- and post-cholesterol mutants here described can now be used as a model system for analyses of the role of SGA, as well as that of other metabolites such as sterols or saponins, in plant defence processes under natural conditions. 

TGA levels were reduced not only in mutant leaves and tubers but also in fibre and protein fractions that had been obtained as by-products in the production process of starch from field-grown tubers (Figure 4). TGA levels in fibre and protein fractions were in four events significantly below the recommended upper safe level of $1 5 0 \mathrm { m g } \mathrm { k g } ^ { - 1 }$ (Byrne 2002) and were in the 16DOX mutant not different from the low TGA levels detected in the normally edible starch preparation from wild-type plants. This strongly suggests that both fibre and protein prepared as by-products from the starch extraction process from low-SGA mutants may become of food grade with no or a minimal need for SGA removal processes. It is in this context interesting to note that the present annual production of starch potatoes within the European Union is ca. 7.5 million tons. Assuming an average tuber protein level of $2 \%$ , the yearly content of potato protein in starch side-streams can thus be estimated to be 150 000 tons. Seemingly, this amount of high-quality plant protein might find both sustainable and profitable use as food and feed, given that SGA levels could be lowered. Our results demonstrate a potential for the production of both fibre and protein of food-grade 

quality from by-products in the starch production process, although larger field experiments will probably be necessary to identify an optimal combination of genetic background, yield, starch level and low SGA in starch by-products. 

In summary, while mutations in all key SGA genes tested led to a significant SGA decrease in both pre- and post-cholesterol mutants, the strategy of knocking out key genes in the postcholesterol pathway proved to be more efficient, particularly in the tubers. The obtained mutants, characterised by SGA-free levels in the tubers, along with a normal phenotype and tuber yield, provide valuable insights for future industrial applications. This includes the development of entirely new food-grade protein and fibre products from industrial side-streams. This transformative approach not only enhances profitability but also contributes to environmental benefits, e.g., energy saving and reduced emissions. 

# 4 | Experimental Procedures

# 4.1 | Plant Materials and Growing Conditions

The starch potato cultivar ‘Kuras’ was used throughout in this study. In vitro shoots were cultured in a controlled chamber under long-day conditions (16-h light/8-h dark) maintained at a temperature of $2 4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (light) or $1 8 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (dark), and with light from fluorescent light bulbs giving a photosynthetic photon f lux of $8 0 \mu \mathrm { m o l }$ photons $\mathrm { m } ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { s ^ { - 1 } }$ . Greenhouse cultivation of plants was performed largely under the same conditions as the controlled chamber conditions, except for light being natural daylight supplemented with mercury lamps (ca. $7 0 \mu \mathrm { m o l }$ photons $\mathbf { m } ^ { - 2 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 } ,$ ). 

Field growth was performed at an experimental plot located at Åsums boställe, Gärds Köpinge, Sweden (Lat 55.959952; Lon 14.132766; WGS84). Seed tubers were planted in April 2023 in 6 m rows, $0 . 7 5 \mathrm { m }$ wide $( 4 . 5 \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } )$ . Plants were treated with fungicides against Downy mildew (e.g., Ranman, Infinito) and Alternaria (e.g., Narita, Propulse) during the growing season, following common agricultural practice for the location. 

# 4.2 | Generation of DNA-Free CRISPR/ Cas9-Mutated Plants

Genomic DNA was extracted from cv. ‘Kuras’ leaf tissue using the GeneJet Plant Genomic DNA Purification Mini Kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham USA) and used for amplification of the target regions in different genes to identify homologous regions between all four alleles. For each gene, two sgRNAs were designed targeting different exons in the allelic homologous regions using CRISPR RGEN Cas-Designer (http://www.rgeno me.net/cas-designer) and CRISPOR (http://crispor.tefor.net/ crispor.py). Gene and protein nomenclature was as described (Merino et al. 2023). 

In vitro propagation, protoplast isolation and shoot regeneration were performed as described by Nicolia et  al.  (2015, 2021). Transfection of ribonucleoproteins (RNPs) to freshly isolated protoplasts was conducted as described in Andersson 

et  al.  (2018) using 0.1 nmol synthetic sgRNA (Synthego, Redwood City, CA, USA) and $5 \mu \mathrm { g }$ TrueCut Cas9 v.2 (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, USA) per target. The transfection into protoplasts was conducted with $2 5 \%$ PEG (PEG 4000, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) for 3 min under room temperature. Only one regenerated shoot was selected from a single callus and transferred to root regeneration. HRFA was performed for the initial screening of mutants in regenerated shoots, followed by Sanger sequencing (Eurofins Genomics, Ebersberg, Germany) as described (Andersson et  al.  2017). The editing efficacy was calculated based on the number of shoots where at least one allele was mutated. Further, the types of mutation identified were classified into three groups: Group 1, full KOs where the indels were outof-frame in all alleles; Group 2, full KOs where at least one allele was an in-frame indel; Group 3, partial mutants with at least one wild-type allele unedited. A list of primers and sgRNAs used in this study is shown in Table S1. 

# 4.3 | Glycoalkaloid Analyses

TLC analysis was used for a rapid screening of SGA levels in 5-week-old in vitro mutated events as described (Simonovska and Vovk 2000), with slight adaptations for the in vitro plant materials. For analyses of mature plants and side-stream products, an LC–MS method using $\mathrm { U } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } ^ { } { \cdot } \alpha$ -solanine and $\mathrm { U } \mathrm { - } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } \mathrm { - } \alpha \mathrm { . }$ - chaconine (IsoLife, Wageningen, The Netherlands) was used. 

Leaf and tuber samples of selected mutants were collected from greenhouse-grown plants at 8 weeks and 6 months, respectively, or from tubers obtained from plants grown in the field. A subset of the tuber samples was subjected to two SGA-inducing conditions: wounding and constant white light exposure, as described (Petersson, Arif, et al. 2013). Transversal cutting and sampling of tubers was as described (Merino et al. 2023). All samples were immediately frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at $- 7 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ before subsequent analyses, including SGA quantification and sterol analyses. 

# 4.4 | Isolation of Tuber Starch, Fibre and Protein Fractions

Potato tubers $5 0 0 \mathrm { g }$ f.w.) were mashed in a juice pulp separator. The starch slurry, including proteins, was separated from fibre by gravity. The fibre fraction was diluted with $5 0 0 \mathrm { m L }$ water, mixed with a mixer and re-extracted. The fibre was concentrated by filtration in a cheese cloth, and the resulting fibre fraction was dried on filter paper overnight. The two starch slurries were pooled and filtered through a $1 2 5 \mu \mathrm { m }$ filter to remove any remaining fibre and washed with $5 0 0 \mathrm { m L }$ water. The filtrate was centrifuged for 3 min at $1 5 0 0 \mathrm { g }$ . The supernatant (PFJ) was collected, and the pellet was resuspended in $5 0 0 \mathrm { m L }$ water. The PFJ was heat-denatured $9 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for $2 0 \mathrm { m i n } ,$ ) and centrifuged for $1 0 \mathrm { { m i n } }$ at $3 0 0 0 g$ to isolate the coagulated protein fraction that was dried overnight at $8 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The resuspended pellet was centrifuged for 10 min at $1 5 0 0 g$ , after which any coloured non-starch material on top of the pellet was discarded. The pure starch pellet was resuspended in $5 0 0 \mathrm { m L }$ water, and the slurry was concentrated and dried as for the fibre fraction. The collected fractions were further extracted for SGA quantification by UHPLC–MS/ 

MS following the same protocol as for the tuber materials described below. 

# 4.5 | Standards and Fine Chemicals

Internal standards for sterol analyses: $L$ -proline- $^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 5 }$ , α- ketoglutarate- $^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 4 } ,$ myristic acid- ${ } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ , cholesterol- $\cdot \mathrm { D } _ { 7 } ,$ were from Cil (Andover, MA, USA) and succinic acid- $\mathrm { \cdot D _ { 4 } }$ , $L$ -glutamic acid-$^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 5 } , ^ { 1 5 } \mathrm { N }$ , putrescine- $\mathrm { \cdot D _ { 4 } }$ 4, hexadecanoic acid $^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 4 } ,$ , $D$ -glucose-${ } ^ { 1 3 } { \bf C } _ { 6 }$ and $D$ -sucrose- ${ } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 1 2 }$ 4, were from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). 

SGA internal standards were ${ } ^ { 1 3 } { \bf C } _ { 3 }$ -caffeine (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), $\propto$ -tomatine (Extrasynthèse, Genay, France) and U-13C-α-solanine and $\mathrm { U } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } ^ { } { \cdot } \alpha$ -chaconine (IsoLife, Wageningen, The Netherlands). Unlabelled SGAs $\scriptstyle { \alpha }$ -solanine, $\propto$ -chaconine and $\propto$ -tomatine) were from Extrasynthèse (Genay, France). Stock solutions of each SGA compound were prepared at a concentration of $1 0 0 \mathrm { n g / \mu L }$ and stored at $- 8 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . A 9-point calibration curve $( 0 . 0 0 5 2 5 { - } 1 0 . 5 \mathrm { n g / \mu L } )$ was prepared by serial dilutions. Mass spectrometry grade formic acid was from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA), and hypergrade LiChrosolv acetonitrile and 2-propanol were from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). 

# 4.6 | Glycoalkaloid Quantification by UHPLC– MS/MS

# 4.6.1 | Metabolite Extraction

For SGA and sterol profiling, $2 0 \mathrm { m g }$ (f.w.) of ground leaf or tuber materials were extracted according to Gullberg et  al.  (2004). In detail, $1 0 0 0 \mu \mathrm { L }$ of extraction buffer $( 2 0 / 2 0 / 6 0 \mathrm { v / v / v }$ chloroform:water:methanol) including internal standards for sterol (L-proline ${ } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 5 }$ , $\propto$ -ketoglutarate- ${ } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 4 }$ , myristic acid- ${ } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 } .$ , cholesterol- $\mathbf { \cdot D _ { 7 } } ,$ 5 succinic acid- $\cdot \mathrm { D } _ { 4 } .$ 4, L-glutamic acid- $^ { 1 3 } { \mathrm C } _ { 5 } , ^ { 1 5 } { \mathrm N }$ , putrescine- $\mathbf { \cdot D _ { 4 } } ,$ , hexadecanoic acid $^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 4 }$ , $D$ -glucose ${ } ^ { 1 3 } { \mathrm { C } } _ { 6 }$ and Dsucrose- $^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 1 2 } )$ and SGA ${ } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ -caffeine and $\propto$ -tomatine) was prepared. The sample was shaken with a tungsten bead in a mixer mill at $3 0 \mathrm { H z }$ for 3 min, after which the sample was centrifuged at $+ 4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , 14 000 rpm $( 1 8 6 2 0 g )$ ), for $1 0 \mathrm { { m i n } }$ . The supernatant was collected and $5 0 \mu \mathrm { L }$ was transferred to micro vials, evaporated to dryness in a speed-vac concentrator for sterol analysis, after which the samples were stored at $- 8 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until analysis. For SGA analyses, $1 0 \mu \mathrm { L }$ of the extract was transferred to a microvial and stored at $- 8 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until analysis. 

Aliquots of the remaining supernatants were pooled and used to create quality control (QC) samples. The QC samples were analysed according to a randomised run order on both sterol profiling and SGA analysis. 

# 4.6.2 | Glycoalkaloid Quantification by LC–ESI–MSMS

Prior to analysis, the final potato extract $( \mathrm { 1 0 } \mu \mathrm { L } )$ was diluted with $7 0 \mu \mathrm { L }$ $\mathrm { U } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } ^ { } { \cdot } \alpha$ -solanine and $\mathrm { U } ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { C } \mathrm { - } \alpha$ -chaconine (IsoLife, Wageningen, The Netherlands) to a final concentration of $0 . 1 \mathrm { n g }$ $\mathrm { S G A } \mu \mathrm { L } ^ { - 1 }$ . Protein and fibre fractions of wild-type samples were 

diluted 1:100 and 1:10 in MilliQ water, respectively. Samples were analysed using a 1290 Infinity system from Agilent Technologies (Waldbronn, Germany), consisting of a G4220A binary pump, a G1316C thermostated column compartment, and a G4226A autosampler with a G1330B autosampler thermostat coupled to an Agilent 6495A triple quadrupole mass spectrometer equipped with a jet-stream electrospray source operating in positive ion mode. 

Separation was achieved by injecting $1 \mu \mathrm { L }$ of each sample onto an Acquity UPLC HSS T3, $2 . 1 \times 5 0 \mathrm { m m }$ , $1 . 8 \mu \mathrm { m } \mathrm { ~ C ~ } _ { 1 8 }$ column in combination with a $2 . 1 \times 5 \mathrm { m m }$ , $1 . 8 \mu \mathrm { m }$ VanGuard precolumn (Waters Corporation, Milford, MA, USA) held at $4 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The gradient eluents used were $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } 0 . 1 \%$ formic acid (A) and 75/25 acetonitrile:2-propanol, $0 . 1 \%$ formic acid (B) with a flow rate of $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { L m i n ^ { - 1 } }$ . The initial conditions consisted of $1 5 \%$ B, and the following gradient was used with linear increments: $0 { - } 1 . 5 \operatorname* { m i n }$ $( 1 5 \% - 2 4 \% \textrm { I }$ B), $1 . 5 \mathrm { - } 6 . 0 \mathrm { m i n }$ $2 4 \% - 2 5 \%$ B), $6 . 0 { - } 6 . 5 \operatorname* { m i n }$ $( 2 5 \% - 4 0 \%$ B), 6.5–9.5 min $( 4 0 \% - 4 8 \%$ B), $9 . 5 \mathrm { - } 1 0 . 0 \mathrm { m i n }$ $( 4 8 \% - 9 9 \%$ B), 10.0– $1 1 . 0 \mathrm { m i n }$ $9 9 \%$ B), $1 1 . 0 \mathrm { - } 1 1 . 3 \mathrm { m i n }$ $( 9 9 \% - 1 5 \%$ B), $1 1 . 3 \mathrm { - } 1 1 . 6 \mathrm { m i n }$ $( 1 5 \%$ B, flow increased to $0 . 8 \mathrm { { m L m i n ^ { - 1 } } }$ , $1 1 . 6 \mathrm { - } 1 2 . 5 \mathrm { m i n }$ $1 5 \%$ B, flow at $0 . 8 \mathrm { { m L m i n ^ { - 1 } } }$ ), $1 2 . 5 \mathrm { - } 1 2 . 7 \mathrm { m i n }$ $1 5 \%$ B, flow decreased to $0 . 5 \mathrm { { m L m i n ^ { - 1 } } }$ ). From 11.3 to $1 2 . 7 \mathrm { m i n }$ , the flow rate was set at $8 0 0 \mu \mathrm { L m i n ^ { - 1 } }$ for faster equilibration of the column. 

Multiple reactions monitoring (MRM) of transitions for the SGAs were optimised using MassHunter MS Optimizer software (Agilent Technologies Inc., Santa Clara, CA, USA) (Table  S4). The fragmentor voltage was set at 380 V, the cell accelerator voltage at 5 V, jet-stream gas temperature was $1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ with a gas flow of $1 6 \mathrm { L / m i n }$ , sheath gas temperature $3 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , sheath gas flow of 11 L/min. The nebuliser pressure was set to 35 psi and the capillary voltage was set at 4 kV. Nitrogen was used as collision gas. The QqQ was run in Dynamic MRM Mode with 2 min retention time windows and 500 ms cycle scans. The data were quantified using MassHunter Quantitation software B08.00 (Agilent Technologies Inc., Santa Clara, CA, USA) and the amount of each SGA species was calculated based on external calibration curves. 

# 4.7 | Sterol Analysis by GC–TOF–MS

For sterol GC–TOF–MS analysis, derivatization was performed as described (Gullberg et al. 2004). A small volume $( 0 . 5 \mu \mathrm { L } )$ of the derivatised sample was injected in splitless mode by a L-PAL3 autosampler (CTC Analytics AG, Switzerland) into an Agilent 7890B gas chromatograph equipped with a $1 0 \mathrm { m } \times 0 . 1 8 \mathrm { m m }$ fused silica capillary column with a chemically bonded $0 . 1 8 \mu \mathrm { m }$ Rxi-5 Sil MS stationary phase (Restek Corporation, U.S.). The injector temperature was $2 7 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , the purge flow rate was $2 0 \mathrm { { m L m i n ^ { - 1 } } }$ and the purge was turned on after 60 s. The gas flow rate through the column was $1 \mathrm { { m L m i n ^ { - 1 } } }$ . The column temperature was held at $7 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 2 min, then increased by $4 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } / \mathrm { m i n }$ up to $3 2 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , and held for 2 min. The column effluent was introduced into the ion source of a Pegasus BT time-of-flight mass spectrometer (GC– TOF–MS) (Leco Corp., St Joseph, MI, USA). The transfer line and the ion source temperatures were $2 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , respectively. Ions were generated by a 70 eV electron beam at an ionisation current of $2 . 0 \mathrm { m A }$ , and 30 spectra $\mathbf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ were recorded 

in the mass range m/z 50–800. The acceleration voltage was turned on after a solvent delay of 150 s. The detector voltage was 1800–2300 V. 

For the GC–MS data, all non-processed MS files from the metabolic analysis were exported from the ChromaTOF software in NetCDF format to MATLAB R2021a (Mathworks, Natick, MA, USA), where all data of pre-treatment procedures, such as baseline correction, chromatogram alignment, data compression and multivariate curve resolution were performed. The extracted mass spectra were identified by comparisons of their retention index and mass spectra with libraries of retention time indices and mass spectra (Schauer et al. 2005). Mass spectra and retention index comparison were performed using NIST MS 2.2 software. Annotation of mass spectra was based on reverse and forward searches in the library. Masses and ratios between masses indicative of a derivatised metabolite were especially noted. The mass spectrum with the highest probability indicative of a metabolite and the retention index between the sample and library for the suggested metabolite was $\pm 5$ (usually less than 3); the deconvoluted “peak” was annotated as an identification of a metabolite.
